text: Several peptide hormones change to an α-helical conformation upon binding to the receptor and there is a great need to develop sensitive assays to diagnose clinical diseases. Although de novo design of proteins that act as high affinity binders for structured targets has been demonstrated, design of interfaces between short peptides that can exist with high α-helical content is an extremely challenging task. Here we show that peptide/protein interaction design, which combines the use of parametric generation and deep learning (based on the RFdiffusion framework and a new method that we name “partial diffusion” where iterative noising and denoising refines the structure of a protein to improve its target binding), results in proteins that give picomolar affinities to their target peptides. These proteins were either optimized from a starting point (e.g. a known antibody surface) or were designed de novo from total randomness. In addition to serving as targeting molecules for increasing the sensitivity of mass spectrometry assays to detect peptides and proteins of interest, they can also serve as modular bioluminescent biosensors, where we show the feasibility of using them to enhance the mass spectrometric detection of parathyroid hormone and glucagon. Overall, design of proteins that target flexible conformational states of peptides or proteins and the development of proteins that benefit from the refinement provided by the partial diffusion process, opens many therapeutic and diagnostic opportunities.
explanation: The text is highly coherent and uses credible domain-specific terms (RFdiffusion, partial diffusion). The inclusion of a new method name makes it hard to distinguish from AI-generated content, so a moderate probability is assigned.
